journal_entry.description: Acquisition of land and building for factory construction, financed by instalment note payable
journal_entry.rationale: Under IFRS, the land is recorded at the present value of the instalment payments using the market interest rate of 6%. The purchase price of $60,000,000 paid in 6 equal annual instalments of $10,000,000 has a present value of $49,173,240 (calculated as $10,000,000 × 4.91732, the present value of an ordinary annuity factor for 6 periods at 6%). The existing building has no separate value since it will be demolished immediately—its cost is included in the land cost as part of site preparation. Under IAS 16, costs of preparing the site for its intended use (including demolition) are capitalized to land. The liability is recorded at present value, with the discount unwinding as interest expense over the payment period. Two asset accounts are used: Land (non-depreciable) captures the site cost, while the demolished building value is implicitly part of land preparation. The debit tuple suggests 2 asset increases, which aligns with recognizing the land acquisition. The credit tuple suggests 1 liability increase for the note payable.

output.reason:
This transaction involves acquiring land for $60,000,000 paid via instalments. On the credit side:
1. Cash payment is NOT made immediately - instead, a liability is created for the deferred payment obligation. The present value of 6 annual instalments of $10,000,000 at 6% interest represents a liability increase (financing liability/notes payable).
2. Under IFRS, when payment terms differ from immediate cash payment, the asset is recorded at present value and a financial liability is recognized. The liability increase is the credit entry.

Since the payment is deferred with instalments, this creates one credit line: liability increase (present value of the payment obligation).

credit_classifier.reason:
This transaction involves acquiring land for $60,000,000 paid via instalments. On the credit side:
1. Cash payment is NOT made immediately - instead, a liability is created for the deferred payment obligation. The present value of 6 annual instalments of $10,000,000 at 6% interest represents a liability increase (financing liability/notes payable).
2. Under IFRS, when payment terms differ from immediate cash payment, the asset is recorded at present value and a financial liability is recognized. The liability increase is the credit entry.

Since the payment is deferred with instalments, this creates one credit line: liability increase (present value of the payment obligation).

output.reason:
Under IFRS, the land acquisition is recorded at present value. The debit side records:
1. Land (asset increase) - The land is acquired for the purpose of constructing a new factory. The existing building to be demolished is part of the land cost, not a separate asset, since it has no future economic benefit and will be immediately demolished. This is recorded at the present value of the payment obligation.
2. Discount on liability (asset increase) - The difference between face value ($60M) and present value (PV of 6 annual payments of $10M at 6%) creates a discount that will be amortized. The discount is a contra-liability, which behaves as an asset increase on the debit side.

Both debit lines are asset increases: the land at PV and the discount on the liability.

Both debit lines are asset increases: the land at PV and the discount on the liability.
